claim: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: execution_policy declares project_mutation forbidden and restricts allowed_write_roots to evidence dispatch output directory only
evidence.why: This is the core contract that preserves tool capability while preventing target project mutation.

evidence.summary: Runtime builds before/after boundary snapshots using buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots, then converts any changes to readiness blockers
evidence.why: This is the enforcement mechanism that detects violations even when agents have full tools.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function normalizes provider selection and model configuration, preventing cross-provider leakage
evidence.why: Lines 437-449 and 450-463 show explicit provider-specific overrides that prevent Claude settings from leaking into Codex or custom providers.

evidence.summary: Claude provider explicitly clears claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, and claude_disallowed_tools to empty strings, relying on boundary enforcement instead of tool restriction
evidence.why: This confirms the doctrine claim that safety comes from prompt contract and boundary snapshots, not from tool flag restrictions.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation constructs provider-specific command line arguments without mixing provider settings
evidence.why: Each provider branch (codex/custom/claude) handles only its own configuration namespace.

evidence.summary: Test proves codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model and preserves independent sandbox configuration
evidence.why: This is evidence that cross-provider model leakage is prevented at the adapter level.

evidence.summary: Test confirms claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement
evidence.why: This validates that full tool capability is preserved while safety comes from the boundary layer.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass
evidence.why: This is evidence that the boundary enforcement works independently of agent output quality.

evidence.summary: Test confirms git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations
evidence.why: This shows the boundary detection works for files that git itself would track.

evidence.summary: Doctrine states Claude provider dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on prompt contract, artifact root, and boundary snapshots for safety
evidence.why: This is the product-level documentation that explains the design intent behind the implementation.

assumptions: The harness boundary detection works for both tracked and untracked project files in git repositories
Git ls-files command succeeds in git-initialized project roots during boundary snapshot
Provider agents respect the prompt contract even when they have full tool capability
risks: If boundary snapshot fails silently, agents could mutate project files without detection
If provider ignores prompt contract and has filesystem write tools, mutations could occur before boundary check
Non-git projects may have weaker boundary detection if walkFiles misses changes in excluded directories

result.notes: All three files in scope_in exist and show coherent implementation. Provider configuration preserves tool capability by design (clearing tool restrictions for Claude, not passing unnecessary flags to Codex/custom), and cross-provider leakage is prevented by provider-specific config namespaces and explicit overrides in executionConfig. The boundary enforcement layer (snapshots + diff + blockers) provides safety independent of tool configuration.
